items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: What does it mean for an allele to be 'dominant'?
items.correct: It is expressed in the phenotype when at least one copy is present (in a heterozygote).
items.misconception: A dominant allele is the most common allele in a population.
items.random_wrong: A dominant allele physically destroys the recessive allele in the genome.
items.why: The everyday meaning of 'dominant' (most prevalent, strongest) misleads students into thinking it relates to frequency or power.

items.correct: They are random changes in DNA — caused by errors in replication or by mutagens — not directed by need.
items.misconception: Mutations occur because organisms need to adapt to their environment.
items.random_wrong: Mutations only happen in organisms that have been damaged by external chemicals.
items.why: Students confuse mutation with adaptation and think evolution involves organisms 'trying' to change — a Lamarckian rather than Darwinian view.

items.q: Do mutations happen because an organism needs to adapt?
items.correct: No — mutations are random changes in DNA; they are not produced on demand to meet a need.
items.misconception: Mutations occur because the organism needs them to survive.
items.random_wrong: Mutations only happen in organisms damaged by chemicals.
items.why: Students think evolution is goal-directed, but mutations arise randomly; selection then acts on whichever happen to be useful.

items.q: Does a 'dominant' allele mean it is the most common allele in a population?
items.correct: No — dominant means it is expressed when present in a heterozygote; it has nothing to do with how common it is.
items.misconception: A dominant allele is the most common one in the population.
items.random_wrong: A dominant allele destroys the recessive allele.
items.why: Students link 'dominant' to frequency, but a dominant allele can be rare; dominance only describes which allele's effect shows in a heterozygote.

items.q: Which bases pair together in DNA?
items.correct: A with T, C with G
items.misconception: A with C, T with G
items.random_wrong: A with G, C with T
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A with T, C with G. “A with C, T with G” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Human body cells contain how many chromosomes?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 46. “23” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Humans have how many pairs of chromosomes?
items.why: Exact answer: 23. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
